The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 1 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 1 of 36
Bob Bly: 	And welcome to the second of five calls in the Clayton Makepeace
“make money as a copywriter” teleconference series. My name is
Bob Bly and we are Wendy Makepeace.
Wendy Makepeace: 	Hello there.
Bob Bly: 	And Clayton Makepeace.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Hi.
Julie McManus: 	Otherwise known as the redhead.
Bob Bly: 	Otherwise known as the redhead. [Laughs] Now, this call is
lengthened. When we – we’re scheduled to do an hour, and we
have lengthened it to 90 minutes. Now, we did that because last
week we thought we were really running behind. But as it turns
out, we were really on track. I thought we were behind for
Call No. 1, which was the subject “how to get every penny you’re
worth,” but we actually covered everything. We talked about the
reasons why all of us deserve the same royalties the big
copywriters get.
We talked about the way to become a power player in the client
relationship, win/win compensation arrangements for every kind of
work you do, how to make the client more profitable for you, how
to make each client more profitable for you by writing more for
them and other secrets. We talked about contracts in detail,
including landmines to avoid in freelance writing copywriting
contracts, and how to collect every penny of the royalties due you.
The second call, which is today, the subject is how to keep your
clients coming back to you over and over again forever. And on
this call, we’ll be focusing on the care and feeding of your clients.
Customer service. Client service. How to keep your clients
coming back to you over and over again with big advance checks
in hand ready to hire you to write more copy.
Now, the items we’re gonna cover on today’s call are as follows:
First, what happens when you get a job for a client and your
package doesn’t work? ’Cause sometimes they don’t. Can you get
hired again? We’re gonna talk about that. What clients say to you
behind your back, and how to turn even the difficult clients into
nice, friendly, easy-to-work-with clients, common blunders that get
WARNING:
The following transcript is raw, uncut, and may contain grammatical errors.

Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 2 of 36
copywriters fired and how to avoid them, how to keep a client in
the revision process from destroying your copy, the kind of
critiques or crits you’ll see and how to handle them, how to
instantly end any copywriting argument and keep your strongest
copy intact, and times when you should demand your kill fee or
kiss the client bye and move on to greener pastures.
And that’s what we’re gonna cover in Call No. 2. So we’re really
not behind, but we’ll use the time. We’re gonna start off with
those items. I’m gonna ask Clayton to start and give us what’s on
that menu, and we’re gonna go through it and cover everything.
And then in the extra time remaining, especially since we’ve
budgeted an extra half hour, we’re gonna get to some of those
great questions that you had emailed us last week that we were not
yet able to get to. Clayton, does that sound like a fair way to
proceed?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sounds good to me.
Bob Bly: 	All right, so let me ask you. The first one really intrigued me. I
get this question all the time from beginning copywriters. And
next to you, I probably get approached and asked questions more
by beginning copywriters I think more than anyone else who’s
around. And the common one is “Well, what happened? You
keep saying when you have the control, the client will love you.
What happens when I get hired to write a package, and I write a
package that either doesn’t beat the control or worse, it’s a bomb?
It doesn’t, you know” – I had a couple people said, “I wrote a
package and they got any orders.” Then you’re dead, right? Then
you’re destroyed at – that client will never hire you again. Isn’t
that right?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Not really. If you’re working with a company that has any
experience in direct mail especially, you’re going to find that
they’re not that demanding. They’re not going to try you once and
then end you. Every good marketing director knows that there’re a
lot of reasons why even brilliant copy sales – and they have a long
experience working with people like you and me and
Gary Bencivenga and Jim Rutz, and they know that even A-level
writers don’t hit ’em out of the park on every try. So if they like
your copy, if they feel it had a visceral appeal, it’s very likely that
they’ll want to come back to you. Just off the top of my head,
there’s six or seven different reasons that have nothing to do with
your talent as a writer or the power of your copy that could be
behind a less-than-satisfactory result, especially in the publishing

-- 2 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 3 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 3 of 36
industry, but also in other industries that you’ll write for. The
main theme of your package, for example, is a key.
You may decide, for example, if you’re writing a financial piece
that the biggest news today is rising energy prices. And you may
focus the opening, the main headline and the opening and the main
theme of your package, on rising energy prices. But between the
time you write the copy and the time it gets in the mail, which can
quite often be months, there may be another theme that comes up
that investors are far more interested in. And perhaps a competitor
or someone else has written on that and so as a result, your copy
doesn’t become the new control. Even in writing for
non-publishing clients, you may choose a particular benefit that
resonates best with you and make that the main theme of your
copy. But you may find out by mailing that that theme doesn’t
resonate as well with your market as the theme that somebody else
has used. So the main theme is a key.
I’m hired a lot of times to launch new products. The client wants
to give the product the very best chance of making it, and so he
goes to writers that have a good track record with him. But
launches are low percentage deals. Well, actually, they can be a
grand slam or they can be a strikeout just as easily. And so quite
often, if it’s a large product, the first time it’s been promoted, it
may just be that the product itself doesn’t resonate with the
marketplace. I’ve had situations where we had an editor of a
newsletter, for example, who was a little odd looking. We did our
launch as we normally would for his product and had his picture on
the front of the magalog and it didn’t work very well.
And so we went back and retested some headlines and we retested
the design without a photograph of the editor of the product, and
we got the control we were looking for. So the product could be
one of the things. Another is the offer. Whenever you’re making
an offer in one of these promotions, you quite often will have free
gifts that you give away. Perhaps the free gift that you chose, the
premiums that you chose to offer, don’t have the same appeal to
the market as the premiums another writer may have chosen. And
in some cases, you have no choice in that matter. Many times
you’re told which free gifts you’re going to give away.
Another problem that I run into quite often, these days especially,
is compliance. Both in the health and financial industries, which
are the areas I work in most, the level of compliance – or let me
make that clearer. There are regulatory agencies that are – and the
next issue of TTP is on this by the way and it’s some good stuff in

-- 3 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 4 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 4 of 36
there for you if you’re a beginning writer and you wanna
understand legal issues. It’s our scary Halloween issue. But in all
industries, the Federal Trade Commission regulates what can be
said and what can’t be said in sales copy. And then in individual
industries there’re also other regulatory bodies that rule on what
you can say and what you can’t say. In the financial areas, the
Securities and Exchange Commission, the Commodity Futures
Trade Commission, and also the National Association of Securities
Dealers.
In the health area, it’s the Food and Drug Administration. And so
each client implements those regulations at a different level and
finds different regulations within those bodies of regulations to be
more important than others. And if there has been a change in how
the client enforces compliance on its copy, or if you’ve chosen a
theme that requires stricter compliance measures than one your
competitor has chosen, you could wind up with weaker copy and
therefore not win. And I just had a situation that was legally
related. It didn’t have to do with regulatory compliance. I wrote
what I thought was kick-butt copy for the package on the dangers
of prescription drugs. And everybody was excited about the copy,
and it led right off with the Vioxx scandal and the fact that as many
as 140,000,000 people have – 140,000 people have had heart
attacks after taking Vioxx for pain relief.
And the attorneys killed all mention of any drug in the package.
Bob Bly: 	You mean you actually can’t use the name Vioxx?
Clayton Makepeace: 	In this case, I couldn’t because the client was concerned about
liability with the –
Bob Bly: 	With Merck –
Clayton Makepeace: 	With Merck. And actually I nailed Pfizer too. And so [Laughs]
’cause I had Celebrex and –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Right, right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Were all in there. And so the client’s own legal parameters can be
a limitation, and they can be more of a limitation on your package
if you choose a theme that flirts with those limitations than on the
control or package somebody else writes.

-- 4 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 5 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 5 of 36
Bob Bly: 	Now, I have to stop and ask you. The immediate thought that
comes into our minds is you are Clayton Makepeace. Did you just
say to the client, “Look. You’re at risk” [Audio Cuts Out]
________ comply, or did you say, “Hey, you’re making a dumb
mistake.” Did you argue at all?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh, when you’re not talking to the lawyer, arguing is fruitless. It’s
pointless because the lawyer’s basically saying, “Look. You know
the copywriter’s probably right about this. But the fact is if you
mail this, we could find ourselves in a lawsuit that’ll cost us
hundreds of thousands of dollars.” And so at that point, unless
you’re best friends with the president of the company, compliance
with both liability measures and also with regulatory measures,
that is a business decision.
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.
Clayton Makepeace: 	There’s no such thing as a totally sanitized sales message. Any
regulator can nail you at any time for any reason. Any consumer
can sue you at any time for any reason. And so there’s no – it’s
not a black and white thing. So each business owner has to make
his own call on where a long line of compliance or legal hiding
under the bed he’s going to do. And they’ve established those
parameters long before you came to the scene, so usually arguing
that is worthless. However, you have two choices at that point.
You can go into the package and you can say, “Look. If I can’t
name these drugs, that was the theme of the whole package. I’m
gonna get killed in the mail if I stick with this theme but can’t
name the drugs. Therefore, let me rewrite the front end of the
package with a different theme.” Or you can say, as I did in this
case, “You know, Vioxx is enough in the news right now that I can
go ahead with this package and say the arthritis drugs that they
gave you” –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	“Triple your risk of a heart attack.” And go that way. And so in
that case, that’s what I did.
Bob Bly: 	So a package might not work because they’re too compliant.
They’re interpreting the compliance regulations very strictly and
following them. Or the offer doesn’t work, like the premiums, or
the product, and the launch is a new product and the market
doesn’t want that product. Or you picked the wrong theme. Let’s

-- 5 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 6 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 6 of 36
say, for a financial package you picked inflation, and oil does
better. Why? What are the other?
That’s four reasons why a package wouldn’t work. What are the
others you had?
Clayton Makepeace: 	For new mailers, the list selects, the media he uses, the people he
sends the sales message to, whether it’s direct mail or print or
Internet or wherever –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	He may not have accurately targeted his market. I’m in a situation
right now where I have a package that I just created for a health
company, and I hired Carline to write the package. And she came
back – Carline is Afro-American. She came back and read – after
reading the research and gave me a package that said basically the
darker your skin, the more at risk you are for heart disease.
Because that really resonated with her and she felt that we could
get a 5% response from anybody on the – from many people on
our file. I on the other hand felt like I wanted a broader appeal. So
I took her copy and we’re gonna mail it.
But we’re gonna test it against another version where we focus on
heart disease up front, something that everybody’s concerned
about. Now, when we test those two packages, I went to the client
and I said, “On Carline’s sell and also for a small part of my sell, I
want you to be sure to include some [Audio Cuts Out] _____ lists.”
Rodale has some great lists on health for Afro-Americans for
example. And I suggested that you include a component of that in
both panels so we can actually measure what kind of lift Carline
gets when she’s talking to ethnic minorities. And I think that’s a
key there. The list selects or the media you’re going to need to be
right for the message. Another thing that has nailed me time after
time again is just timing.
If you’re in the mail with a package about how inflation – as I was
back in 1979, if you’re in the mail with a package that talks about
how inflation is going to go through the roof and every dollar you
have is only gonna be worth a penny and all of that kind of thing,
and then Ronald Reagan gets elected president and you go into the
mail, you’re gonna get killed because your constituents, the people
that you’re talking to, feel like they’ve just solved the inflation
problem. And we saw 9/11 kill direct mail response. The
beginning of the wars in Iraq and Kuwait killed response. The
O.J. Simpson trial killed response. People were watching their

-- 6 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 7 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 7 of 36
televisions instead of reading the mail, and so any world event of
any consequence can kill response to a mail package.
And then finally, you don’t know what else is gonna be in the
mailbox, or what other people are gonna be reading when your
package arrives. And so if a competitor, another company, is
mailing a package on the same theme, or another company has
come up with another theme or another headline concept that’s
extremely strong, and your package and their package are on the
same day, you may find that your response is less than you’d
hoped. So these are just – that’s just seven things very quickly that
major mailers know affect response. And so another one might be
the graphic design of the piece. They say that people who love
sausage or direct mail should never see either one being created.
The direct mail stuff that we do, whether it’s copy or design, is a
messy process. It’s a creative process.
And it’s very hard to get a consistent product out of graphic
designers especially. And I’ve seen situations where changing the
design of a package increased response 20 to 30%. So you’re not
the only one here involved in this. The list broker, the manager of
the media plan, the graphic designer, the guy who designed the
product before the editor, all these people have input into the
success or failure of a promotion.
Bob Bly: 	So you are saying you believe that a sophisticated client knows
this, and therefore if they liked your package but it doesn’t work,
and they feel that one of these factors could’ve made that so, they
will try you again?
Clayton Makepeace: 	All of the things being equal, yes. Yeah, so long as the
relationship was good. So long as you were responsive. And some
of the things we’ll get to in a minute. So long as you handle the
client in a professional way. So long as they felt your copy was
strong, you’re not going to be axed just for that.
Bob Bly: 	Well, let’s talk about that. ’Cause you often hear some people say,
“If you can write a winner, the world is your oyster and nothing
else matters. If you write killers.” But you’ve said, or you’re
hinting, that you can write winners, but if there’s other problems
like you’re a great copywriter but you’re kind of a jackass to
people, they’re not as eager to hire you. Does it matter how, you
know, the customer service aspect of it? Or is it just what you
write?

-- 7 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 8 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 8 of 36
Clayton Makepeace: 	No, no. Everything I’ve said about these people, just their lives
rising and falling on finding good writers, is absolutely true.
However, there is a line that they won’t go over, right? I could
name one guy right now, and there’re others, who’s an excellent
writer and who’s kicked out control after control for 30 years now,
but nobody wants to hire him because he’s so hard to work with.
One of the things I think you’ll find as you meet top copywriters is
when you meet top freelancers, they’re invariably nice people. If
you’ve ever talked to Gary Bencivenga, he’s unassuming, nice,
wants to help you, just one of the kindest people you’ll ever meet.
Same as with Jim Rutz.
I like to think that I’m fairly open and helpful with the information
and advice I give. The ones who’ve risen to the top of the field are
not egotistical, my-way-or-the-highway kind of people. They’re
the kind of people that will work with you and be a very helpful
part of the team. Rather than somebody that says, “Look, you
either mail this or forget it.” So now the guy I was thinking about,
his copy is legendary. But he hasn’t been able to build a freelance
copywriting business of any consequence because he’s so hard to
work with. As a result, he’s making millions developing and
selling his own products, so no harm, no foul as far as he’s
concerned.
But the clients that all of us are working for just will not work with
him.
Bob Bly: 	So if you’re difficult with a client, is it a multiple affect? Will they
keep it to themselves, or do these guys talk to each other?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh, they talk to each other all the time. In our business especially,
Bob, it’s very – as you know, it’s very incestuous. They have
something called the publishers roundtable. They actually go to
some city in Europe or the United States once a year and hang out
together for three or four days and share notes on copywriters.
And the marketing managers call each other up on the phone and
ask ’em about lists that’re working and compare notes on
copywriters. So people are talking all the time. And because
copywriters are a key part of this equation, we tend to be a topic of
conversation.
And again, this isn’t just in the health and financial publishing
area, or just in the vitamin sales area, you get involved in anything
else, any other niche, whether it be catalogues or even a niche in a
niche. You know, a particular kind of catalogue marketing, or
anything else that you’re doing, there is an industry. There is an

-- 8 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 9 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 9 of 36
association, and these people talk to each other. And plus, if
they’re smart, they’re selling each other’s products too. So word
will get around.
Bob Bly: 	So if I’m working with a finite group of clients and they’re talking
about me behind my back, what are the things I can do to make
them when they talk about me say nice things other than write
winning copy?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, this is one of the things that we promised to discuss on this
call. I’d say the first thing is it’s essential that you be prepared.
The minute that you get a signed contract, the next thing that’s
going to happen is that the client is gonna wanna have a conference
call with you, or perhaps in some cases a live meeting with you
and with other key people on their team. It could be the person
who invented the product. It could be the editor of the newsletter.
It could be the [Audio Cuts Out] ___________ of the vitamin.
And then also the marketing people who’ve been involved with the
product.
These people can be solid gold for you if you know how to listen
to what they say. Because they’ve been involved or at least have
access to hundreds of tests over the years where they’ve tested one
kind of headline against another, one kind of design against
another, one kind of theme against another. And these people are
gonna be solid gold to you if you know how to listen to them. A
lot of them will not say, “We tested XYZ back in such-and-such a
year and these are the results.” But what they’ll say is something
much vaguer, but they’re trying to tell you, “Look, we tried this.
We know how well it works.”
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And so those people are key. So anyway, so after you sign the
contract, you’re going to have this meeting. It’s very important
that you be prepared for that meeting, that you are very familiar
with the product, that you’re very familiar with the promotions that
they’ve used in the recent past, and that you have some grasp or
handle on what their competitors are using right now to promote
their products. And so in preparation for that meeting, I usually
ask for in the newsletter area a year of back issues, copies of the
free gifts that they give away with the sale of the newsletter, and I
ask for a biography on the editor. If it’s a three-dimensional
product or a supplement, I ask for research on the supplement so I
can take a look at what it contains, what it does, and how
competitors are selling a similar supplement. There’re two-fold

-- 9 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 10 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 10 of 36
benefits to that. The first part of the benefit is you’re gonna know
what you’re talking about when you have that launch meeting.
The second one is that the people who you’re working will be
impressed with your professionalism. They’ll be put at ease.
They’ll say, “Wow, this guy seems to really know what he’s
doing.” So I think that’s very important. Following along the
same lines, the second thing to do is at the meeting, as you’re
brainstorming, the question will always come up, “What do you
need to write this package?” And I think it’s really important to
know what you’re going to ask for because again, that reflects on
your professionalism. I always ask for other promo copy they’ve
used, both successful and unsuccessful, and for them to tell me
which is which.
I can usually guess, but not always. So I wanna know that. And
it’s surprises that can really give you a leg up. When you see
something and you say, “Wow, this really stinks.” And then you
find out that was a big winner for them, then that’s a clue. That’s a
clue right there that you need to go farther than your own gut
instinct on the product.
Bob Bly: 	Let me ask you. Let me stop you and ask you something that I’m
asked all the time. Let’s say you’re doing this and the client gives
you a bunch of promotions for this product, and you notice that all
the winners are kind of promotional looking jumbos, 9 x 12
envelopes.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	And all the losers are No. 10 letter packages, magalogs, tabloids.
They all lost.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	Would you be smarter to again try something different than all
their winning packages. Which are all the same; they’re all
jumbos. Or would you be better staying in that format?
Clayton Makepeace: 	That’s a great question. That’s something that comes up at the
beginning of this process every single time. I used to struggle with
it tremendously. And, you know, “What do I do here? Do I do a
6 x 9? Do I do a No. 10? Do I do a tabloid or a magalog?”
My philosophy now is go with what’s working on your first outing.
If the winner is a tabloid, then go with a tabloid. What you’re

-- 10 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 11 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 11 of 36
counting on is that the power of your copy is going to be stronger
than the power of the other guy’s copy. And so if you introduce
another variable into the mix, if he’s doing an 8-1/2 x 11 special
report style format piece that’s 24 pages long, and you write a
24-page tabloid, well, you’ve just introduced a new variable that’s
to your detriment. Because that tabloid cost more to mail so that
means you have to get a higher response to break even, and that
puts you at a disadvantage. So I tend to emulate what’s working
now. And as soon as I get a control on a product, I immediately
look at the format and see if there’re ways to save money on it.
I just had this experience with Boardroom. I wrote a promotion for
one of their books. Arthur Johnson, who’s a great writer, has their
control. Arthur’s control was a tabloid-size 16-pager. My copy
that was challenging his control was a 24-page package. I pulled a
higher response rate and a higher closing rate than Arthur did, but
my piece cost more. So his remained the control.
So then I had to go back and pare my copy down so that I could get
my costs down to where Arthur’s was. Now, at that level, so long
as I haven’t hurt my response rate, and I don’t think I have, I’ll
probably knock off Arthur’s control. So I didn’t learn the lesson
on that. Had I learned my lesson and not been so cocky, I
would’ve looked at Arthur’s thing and I would’ve said, “Look,
man. This guy’s got a 16-page tabloid size control. That’s what
I’m gonna write, and I’m just gonna assure that I do everything I
can possibly do to make my copy [Audio Cuts Out] __________
than his.” I think that’s a much smarter choice.
And I’m famous for going back then after the control is established
and slicing and dicing the copy in different ways to turn it into a
bookalog, a 6 x 9, another format. So I’m taking my risk with
something that’s already proven.
Bob Bly: 	But to do this thing, you’ve gotta know what to ask the client for.
In other words, you should ask them for what they would expect
any competent qualified writer would ask for. And the two basic
things to ask for is the actual product, supplements, newsletters or
whatever, and the promotions. The control and tests, right?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	What else would you ask for?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I also ask for – and they expect this. I think it’s a blunder when
you don’t. Raw testimonials. Most mailers – again, if you’re

-- 11 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 12 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 12 of 36
starting with somebody who isn’t really hip on mailing and stuff
and Internet promotion, they may or may not have testimonials to
work with. But most major mailers do. Most people who’ve been
in the mail any time do have testimonials. I always ask for raw
testimonials.
That’s what the customer said word for word, warts and all. It can
be grammatically incorrect. It can have horrible misspellings in it.
It can ramble on for pages. I don’t care. I wanna see it all,
because I don’t wanna trust somebody else to have edited that
testimonial and to really have drawn the meat out of it. And so the
Federal Trade Commission allows you to edit testimonials so long
as you don’t change their meaning.
And that gives you a lot of latitude in carving these testimonials
down in a way that will really have great impact. And so I don’t
trust that to other people. Another thing is public relations output.
A lot of these companies use PR agents to get press releases out
there and get mentions in the media. There’s nothing more
credible – there’s no more better [Laughs] – there’s no better
credibility boost than the fact that you’re client has been featured
in the Wall Street Journal or major health publications, Prevention
magazine or some place else, or that people call him or call them
on a regular basis for insights on the news or things like that. So
you’ve seen these little “as seen on TV” stickers that they put all
over –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Everything that’s been promoted on television. What they’re
saying there is that, “Look, this is a large credible company. We
have national coverage, and so you can believe what we tell you
about this product.” So the PR output is very important. In
Martin Weiss’ things, I’ve roped in the PR output into three major
sidebars that I ran in his promotion. The first one was just pictures
of him on Nightline and CNBC and on Fox News talking with
Neil Cavuto or Ted Koppel or these other people and talked about
how the – just with a short blurb that says the year. You know,
1989 Martin D. Weiss goes on Nightline to warn people about the
insurance crisis.
So and then I had like eight or nine of those in a box, so it was
media appearances. The next one was actual quotes from the
media about Weiss. That these were just little short one-liners for
[Audio Cuts Out] ____________ says this. Fortune says this, and
Barron’s says that, and so forth. Another sidebar. I was fortunate

-- 12 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 13 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 13 of 36
enough to have a client who had been called to testify before
Congress about a problem in the insurance industry. And so I had
pictures of him on Capitol Hill testifying before a bunch of
Congress people.
All of these things were tremendous credibility builders in my
packages. And then I also ran sidebars showing one or two
paragraphs of text from either an article that Martin had
contributed to a publication, or that some publication had said
about Martin. So PR output is important. If you have it, use it.
We talked about the most successful promotions competitors are
fielding for a similar product. But also look outside of that a little
bit. Ask for any direct mail that’s come in in the last three or four
months that their people have collected that’s impressed with.
Stuff that they think that there might be some application. At
Weiss they have enormous files of this kind of material, and many
other [Audio Cuts Out] ____________. Then articles, books or
other things that’ve been authored by the client. In my area of
health and finance, the editors of our newsletters are quite often
bestselling authors. I’ve done packages for Barry Sears with the
Zone Diet, Deepak Chopra and all of these guys. And I require the
client send me everything that they had written so that I could read
it and really familiarize myself with the tone of the author’s voice,
as well as his message. And then finally, the complete research hit
on whatever theme you’ve chosen as your lead theme for the
package.
Some clients are better at this than others, but almost every
publishing client at least has editorial people who do the research
for the regular issues of the newsletter like the premiums. And so I
ask for those people to help me. Let’s say, for example, I’m doing
a promotion on the emergence of China and India as the world’s
greatest economic powers. Well, I don’t wanna spend hours and
hours online looking up that stuff and reading it all. If they have a
researcher there, chances are he’s already done the research for
articles or premiums have been done, and would be willing to do
more, knows right where to go. They have the ability to do the
LexisNexis searches, and they’re just good to go. It’ll save you a
lot of time on each job, and in early going will also enhance
perception of professionalism that the client has of you.
Bob Bly: 	So to make the client say nice things when they’re talking about
me, I wanna be prepared. I wanna ask them not only for what they
expect me to ask them. If I don’t, they’ll view that I don’t know
the way this is done?

-- 13 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 14 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 14 of 36
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, yeah. It’s gonna help you in that you’ll have more material
to start with so your first draft will be more on target. But it’s also
going to put them at ease and feel that, “Wow, I’m working with a
pro here. He knows exactly what he needs from me.”
Bob Bly: 	Makes sense. What else can I do to make a good impression with
my new client?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, if they don’t offer being with the designer of the product or
the author, then I would request it. I would say, “I wanna dive into
this. You’ve given me a lot of great stuff, but I really wanna pick
this guy’s brain. I wanna get a sense of his passion, and get to
know his personality a little bit.” Some clients have flown their
main guys in to see me. Other times I have to go see them. But
it’s a very helpful time.
And I always ask the question, “Why are you doing this?”
Because I’m looking for passion, and I’m looking for a
commitment between the editor and the prospect. The old “I’m not
gonna let you fail” kind of thing. “I got in this because it breaks
my heart to see people dying of unnecessary heart disease.” Or “I
got into this because I just saw too many people get waxed in the
tech rack and lose their life savings, and I vowed right then and
there I would never let that happen again if there was anything I
could possibly do to stop it.” And so I’m looking for passion and
personality in that meeting, and also for a sense of the possible
theme ideas going forward that the editor is most interested in. Or
in the case of a supplement product, the developments in the
industry that he’s most excited about, that the formulator’s most
excited about. So request that meeting and have half a dozen
starter questions available to kind of work through all of that with
the designer of the product or the editor.
Bob Bly: 	So request a meeting with the product developer or designer.
Great. What else can I do to do a good job for the client but also
show them that I’m a real professional?
Clayton Makepeace: 	All right. There’re two more things that I do. I like to provide a
platform. Now, other people hate doing this. Carline Cole won’t
do it. She says, “It takes me as long to write the whole package as
it does to write the platform.” So she says, “Pay me double and I’ll
give you a platform.”
“Otherwise, I’ll just write the copy.” Parris on the other hand will
write the first – Parris Lampropolis will write the first several

-- 14 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 15 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 15 of 36
pages of the package and send it through, just to get an early take
on the tone and main theme from the client. I like to, after having
the meetings with the group and with the editor or the formulator
or whatever, I like to sit down and put together just
flow-of-consciousness stuff on what I’m thinking about the
product and the client, and what I’m thinking about the – what
shape the promotion piece will ultimately take. I send that through
to the client and say, “Here are my preliminary thoughts. I’d like
you to react to each one of them and let me know how you feel.”
Bob Bly: 	So in your case, you could either create a platform, which is a
proposal or description of the process you have in mind to write, or
as in Parris’ case, he gives them the actual copy. He gives them
two or three pages of copy.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Extremely rough flow-of-consciousness, “Here’s kinda where I
wanna go” –
Bob Bly: 	“Here’s what I wanna do” –
Clayton Makepeace: 	“What do you think?” And it’s their responses to that that’s gonna
give you some clues that’ll save you a lot of time on second and
third drafts.
Bob Bly: 	Now, your next comment about what to do to wow a client is
something that I, being not a people person, have really done
horribly. But you say communicate with the client at unexpected
times. Why? Aren’t these people busy?
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughs] Yeah, they are. But the whole time they’re busy, they’ve
got this little thing in the back of their minds, “Gee, I wonder how
that copy’s going.”
Bob Bly: 	Uh huh.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And –
Bob Bly: 	You’re right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	And they don’t wanna bother you because they know you’re busy.
They don’t wanna interrupt your workflow. They don’t want you
to feel pressured because they want quality. And they know if
you’re thinking about a deadline, they’re gonna get the copy on
deadline but it’ll probably stink. If you’re thinking about the copy,
whether it comes in on a deadline or not, it’s gonna be strong. So

-- 15 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 16 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 16 of 36
they don’t want you to feel rushed or pushed. At least a smart
client doesn’t.
So just dropping an email to them, “Hey, it’s going great.” Or,
“Hey, I found this new web page with some great new information
on it.” Or, “I have some new headlines that I just think are
fantastic. It’s going great.” Confirm when you think that you’ll
have the copy to them. The first-draft copy. Or just call ’em up on
the phone and say, “Hey, how’s it going? I thought I’d touch bases
and wanna let you know I’m really having a lot of fun with this
subject.”
“It’s coming along, and I think it’s gonna be gangbusters.” And
just check in with them from time to time. And you do that,
they’re going to – first of all, think about it from their position.
They hire you. They give you thousands of dollars in an advance
fee. They clear an opening for you in their all-important mail
schedule that will be vacant if you don’t deliver on time. And then
they’re supposed to sit there and cool their heels for three weeks,
four weeks, five weeks, six weeks, while you’re supposedly
working on their copy.
And they have no input from you whatsoever. Calling in even
with questions, or certainly the things that I’ve just mentioned,
goes a long way towards putting them at ease and feeling, “Wow,
this is a comfortable professional relationship.”
Bob Bly: 	So to establish – another tip for establishing yourself as a good
professional, someone they could be happy they hired, is client
contact.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think so. And don’t be afraid to ask questions. A lot of people –
if you go in there under this guise that “I am the world’s greatest
copywriter and I’m gonna save your company and I’m gonna write
this big control and I know everything there is to know,” and you
go in there like that, first of all you got a credibility problem.
’Cause if they’ve been around for a long time, they know there is
no such thing as the world’s greatest copywriter.
The second thing that you’ve got going against you is that they’re
going to feel uncomfortable making suggestions or sharing ideas
they’ve had with you. If instead you approach this as being a
member of a team that you’re excited to be on, and you talk to
them and you pick their brains, and you find the people who’re on
the team, maybe the assistant marketing people or even down
lower on the ladder, and you just call ’em up and you ask ’em a

-- 16 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 17 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 17 of 36
couple of questions, you’re gonna strike gold in a lot of those
cases. And you gotta remember. These are corporate animals.
They all have managers. They all have meetings once a week
where they’re supposed to be able to tell their bosses how the
copy’s coming, and they don’t have a clue.
Bob Bly: 	Now, here’s what people are nervous about. They’re saying,
“What happens if I think I’m doing a good job, but people – what
happens if the client doesn’t hire me back or they fire me?” What
are the common reasons why clients get so unhappy that they stop
doing business with a copywriter, or even kick ’em off a job?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think the first one is really chemistry. And it’s that whole thing
of if you try to make it – I say quite often, first you make a friend,
then you make a sale. Well, it’s the same – and that’s true with
copy, but it’s also true with dealing with clients. The chemistry is
important, and doing little things in order to allow the people
internally to feel that you value them, you value their experience,
and that you’re willing to listen to their ideas I think is very
important. Another one is overpromising and underperforming. If
you think it’s gonna take you three weeks to get the copy done, tell
’em four and deliver in three. I’m not saying lie to ’em.
I’m just saying I tend to be the eternal optimist. Wendy’s always
talking about how I describe something that needs to be done. Say,
“It’s easy. Just do this, that and the other thing.” Well, it’s not
easy. It’s never easy. It always take longer than I think it will.
So allow for that, so that when you can deliver, you’re delivering
something within the timeframe if at all possible. Now, quality of
the copy is more important than the deadline to me though. And if
it’s not there on the deadline, then I communicate. I’ll say, “It’s
not quite there. I need another week.” Or whatever. I try to keep
the client up-to-date.
But again, that attitude is very important, that “I’m here to help
you, and I want to accommodate you.” A lot of times people
internally will actually sandbag or sabotage you. They feel that
you’re minimizing their importance to the process. So there are
those people out there, and the best way to neutralize them is to
bring them into the process and make them feel valued. Another is
failure to listen. And I think this comes back to the fearlessness
that goes along with feeling free to ask questions. I can’t tell you
how many times I’ve brought in younger copywriters to work with
a client and the client and I both tell him what’s important to the
client, what are the themes that have worked in the past.

-- 17 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 18 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 18 of 36
“We’ve tested all these things and this worked better than that,”
and so forth. But then the copy comes back and it’s like we never
had that conversation. So I think it’s really important to pay
attention to the things that they’re telling you. And you can read
between the lines in an attempt to deliver something that’ll be very
close on first draft. The chemistry thing. I wanna go back to that
for a second. That’s just really not something where you can go
away and you can say, “Well, that person’s just a jerk.”
If you weren’t able to salvage a client with your own native charm,
then it’s not that person’s fault. There’re people everywhere that
are not gonna get along with you. Maybe you look like an aunt
that once spanked ’em or an uncle that got ’em in trouble once or
something. You can’t help that. But as a copywriter, as somebody
who provides service to a company, it’s your job to overcome that.
And so identify people that are dealing with – who are difficult and
to find ways to win them over.
Bob Bly: 	How could you win over someone who was difficult, let’s say cold
to you, answering you in monosyllables, and you can tell they
don’t like you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	[Laughs] Well, sometimes it’s not possible, but I think making the
effort is important. And many times, I found out that that person I
thought who didn’t like me was just intimidated, or that person
who didn’t like me was new and felt like – and just needed
someone to say, “Hey look, I think you’re great. I think you’re
doing a great job. You give me a lot of ideas here.” And copy
their boss on the email when you say that. Just understand that
people skills are important. They’re an important process here,
and that any relationship that’s strained, where other members of
the team aren’t really contributing to you and helping you,
weakens your overall effort so –
Bob Bly: 	So look for the chemistry. Be aware of the chemistry, and try to
fix it if you can. Make sure you listen. And don’t overpromise
and then underdeliver?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	What other big blunders do copywriters make – get them the boot?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think the – one of the things that I run into with young writers all
the time is that when the copy comes in, they’re apologizing for it.
They say, “Well, I didn’t quite get the __________ [Audio Cuts

-- 18 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 19 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 19 of 36
Out] right,” or “I don’t like this argument,” or “I don’t really
believe that” or –
Bob Bly: 	A lot of caveats and bewares –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. When I present my copy, I try to make it a gala event. I
don’t oversell it. I don’t say, “Here’s the best package that you’ll
ever see.” But I let them know that I’m proud of the copy, that I’m
proud of the effort, and I have high hopes for it. I also make sure
that when they look at my copy, it looks like it was prepared with
care.
It’s not all different sizes of type and colors and things all over the
page. Clients tend to see that kind of thing and think, “This guy’s
covering up for the fact that he thinks the copy’s weak, and so he’s
gotta go through and he’s gotta make this paragraph bigger than
that paragraph, and he’s gotta do all of these other crazy things
almost trying to be a graphic designer in presenting a copy.” I’ll
run my main heads in 72-point type if I want to in my first draft,
then my running test is all 12-point Times. My subheads are all
14-point Impact. My sidebars are all 12-point Arial or Helvetica or
Veranda. And with the subheads – with the headlines and the
sidebars at 20-point Times bold, and that’s it.
Bob Bly: 	What type is the 72-point headline in?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Say what?
Bob Bly: 	What type is the main headline in –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Oh, I tend to go with Impact because it’s a nice big fat sans serif
condensed face. Because it’s condensed, I can get more letters on
a line with it, which gives you a bigger headline size, right?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Now, usually my rule of thumb is if my headline is 36-point and –
I’m looking for no more than three lines –
Bob Bly: 	Right –
Clayton Makepeace: 	For a headline, right?
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.

-- 19 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 20 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 20 of 36
Clayton Makepeace: 	So if it’s 36-point, that’s an okay headline. If it’s 48-point, that’s a
pretty good headline. If I can do it in 72-points, that means now
I’ve just done a two-word headline, maybe six letters.
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And I mean that’s the best there is. [Laughs] That’s like firing off
a gun behind somebody –
Bob Bly: 	I just looked at –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	I just looked at Impact. It’s cool. I’ve noticed that you do that.
I’ve been using Arial black, but Impact’s a very nice headline type.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep.
Bob Bly: 	So you wanna make it look good. What about typos? Is that
acceptable? Does it matter?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, all I worry about on my drafts, they’re not expecting us to be
copy editors or proofreaders. I tell the story that one time when I
delivered copy, I had an email – or actually it was a letter that I
sent along with it, and I said that this has not been preefred yet – I
misspelled the word proofread –
Bob Bly: 	They thought you were being funny?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. So they thought I was being hilarious, so I’ve used it ever
since. [Laughs] But it was a mistake the first time.
Bob Bly: 	Yeah –
Clayton Makepeace: 	I just run a good spellchecker on it. I turn off the thing in Word
that checks your grammar. That thing’s just –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Yeah, I don’t use it –
Clayton Makepeace: 	But I just do a good spellcheck, and then I go through and make
sure that it reads well. I don’t care about the – what is it? The UPI
or AP rule?
Bob Bly: 	Right.

-- 20 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 21 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 21 of 36
Clayton Makepeace: 	Sometimes I’ll use the number three as a big old number and
sometimes I’ll spell it. It’s just depends –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	So as long as it looks good.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah.
Bob Bly: 	It’s professional. It looks like it was professionally presented,
prepared, and you don’t apologize for it? You hand in something
that you think is good –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I’ve had copywriters where I wanted a 24-page piece from
them and that requires 32 pages single-space roughly. And using
the parameters I just outlined, 12-point running text and so forth,
it’ll be about 32 pages long in order to fill a 24-page magalog.
And so these guys send me copy, but all the running text is
16-point. Well, that just insults my intelligence. They have
written way too short, and they’re trying to cover up for it by
running everything in large type. So don’t insult their intelligence.
Make [Audio Cuts Out] ___________ that your finished product is
professionally presented.
Bob Bly: 	Any [Audio Cuts Out] _____ mistakes I wanna avoid making?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I like to keep – some copywriters will send a copy in with two or
three different leads right up front.
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And I think that presents the client with a decision to make too
early in the process. What I do is I like to pick what I think is my
strongest lead and put it up front so that the client can read my
strongest headline [Audio Cuts Out] __________ copy, my
opening copy, and then all the way through my running text
without interruption. Then after the last line of my sales letter or
running text in the case of a magalog, I’ll have a section that says
sidebars. And in that section then I will have each sidebar that’s
going to punctuate that running text with an approximation of
where it goes. You know, “This goes with the copy. This goes on
Page 2. This is the bio.
This goes with the running text on such-and-such and [Audio Cuts
Out] ___________ all the way through. And then finally I have

-- 21 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 22 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 22 of 36
my response device and back cover in the case of a self-mailer.
And so they can go all the way through in sequence and really
gauge the copy. Then if I have other leads or other headlines, then
I add them to the very tail-end of the document. And I do that
even though I may not be in love with them.
Bob Bly: 	Um hm.
Clayton Makepeace: 	They may be alternates. They may be things I’d like to test. Or
they may just trigger ideas for discussion as we’re going over the
second draft later – going after that draft later.
Bob Bly: 	Got it. Now, it’s five of 1:00, but folks today we’re not ending at
1:00 [Audio Cuts Out] ________ ending [Audio Cuts Out]
________. We have until 1:30, and we let you know that so we
have plenty of time and we’re not rushed. So don’t feel panicked.
You can just sit back, relax, take another sip of the coffee. We
have at least another half hour with Clayton. So let me move on to
the next topic.
How to keep a client from killing your copy. Four times of
critiques you’ll see most often, and how to handle it. And this is
something that I hear from people all the time. I’ll get freelancers
who’ll email me, “I handed in this copy to the client and they say
they hate it.” And the freelancer feels they’re dead in the water.
They don’t know what to do. They’re emotionally upset.
The whole thing’s falling apart.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah. Well, in copywriting as in life, your emotions are probably
your worst enemy. First thing that a young copywriter has to get
used to is this putting yourself out there and having the response to
your copy being less than you’d hoped. Some of the writers I work
with are absolutely crushed emotionally by that, and they get over
it. But it takes courage. It takes guts to pour your life into a piece
of copy for a period of time, send it off to a bunch of mere
strangers, and basically lay yourself bare and say, “Go ahead, cut
my heart out. Do whatever you want to.”
“Tell me it stinks if you want to.” I mean it takes courage to
[Audio Cuts Out] ___________ that vulnerable to somebody.
What takes real fortitude is when the response comes back and it’s
not what you had hoped, and the – I don’t remember the last –
well, yes I do. I do remember the last time somebody said, “We
don’t like it.” It was just a couple of weeks ago. [Laughs] And it
was Chris Moret at [Audio Cuts Out] ______________. I had

-- 22 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 23 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 23 of 36
written a promotion piece for one of his financial editors, and I had
met with financial editor and talked with him, and none of the
people from Phillips were in that meeting.
And the financial editor basically did a brain-dump for me. I then
took that and turned it into a platform that I was discussing earlier
and sent it to the group at Phillips, and they loved it. They had a
couple of very minor comments on it. I then created the package
along the lines of that platform, and I got the call back that said,
“We don’t like it.” And my response to that was simply, “Well,
what don’t you like?” And they said, “Well, we think that your
approach here is a little too general. We’d like to have something
that’s more specific. You’re talking about how Wall Street rips off
investors, and we’d like to have something more topical.”
“Okay. All right. Fine.” It would’ve been nice to have heard that
earlier, but since I hadn’t, I just dove back into the copy and I
created the second draft. And they bought it just with no changes.
So it’s a process. Get used to the idea that it is a process, and that
it can be messy.
Bob Bly: 	So what are the four types of critiques that you see most often?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay. Well, the one that you just mentioned almost never
happens. It almost never happens that they say, “Well, we just
don’t like it.” So you’ll get four kinds of critiques. The first one is
where the client identifies a problem that does not exist in the text,
and then suggests a solution that would actually hurt your
response. If a client or a marketing manager is particularly
detail-oriented, they may come with this kind of critique. And if
the problem doesn’t exist – like, for example, they’ll say, “Well, I
think people who’ll read this might think that.”
Well, let’s face it. People who read this are gonna think a lot of
things. And quite often, that kind of a crit is just somebody
wanting to have something to say. It’s not really even a crit. But
when you identify something where the client says there’s a
problem, you don’t see the problem, and he’s suggesting a solution
that’s gonna hurt, then you just need to get him on the phone at
that point and – let me back up just a second if I might. I think it’s
important when you begin the process to set the ground rules for
critting. In fact, I do it even before I accept the assignment in the
first place.
And my ground rules are that if you – when you have critiques or
suggestions for my copy, that’s what they are. They’re

-- 23 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 24 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 24 of 36
suggestions. They’re not changes. They’re suggestions. “And
you’re hiring me to write this and so then I will make the decision
as to whether your suggestion is one that warrants inclusion in my
copy or not.” If we reach a point where we can’t agree on that,
then there needs to be somebody we can appeal to who’ll make the
ultimate decision. And it needs, of course, to be the person who’s
gonna ultimately put his money behind this.
But in terms of marketing managers and other people that you
work with on a daily basis, they’re hiring you because you’re the
expert. If they were the expert, they could write it themselves.
And so other than legal issues, ethical issues, or issues of fact, I
make sure that everyone understands that I get to make the other
decisions. They can make decisions about fact, legal issues and
ethical issues. But other than that, I’m the copywriter. So having
prepared the way that way, then when I get crits in that I think are
gonna hurt, it’s very easy to pick up the phone and say, “Hey look,
I don’t think this is a problem, and I just don’t wanna make a
change here. And here’s why it isn’t a problem.”
And have the conversation with them. Nine times out of ten,
they’ll let you win. The second kind of crit is when the client
identifies a problem that doesn’t exist, but a solution would be a
strong addition to the copy. The love [Audio Cuts Out]
___________ you know, ’cause any idea, no matter how it’s
derived, is something that’s gonna put money in my pocket. So I
tend to just congratulate them for being brilliant, and just go
straight to work figuring a way to really make that work, to
leverage the power of the new idea. The third way is when a client
identifies a problem that does exist, but the solution stinks. On
those I just say – I validate their observation.
I say, “You know what? You’re right. That slipped past me.
That’s a problem. That’s something I wanna change, but let me
think about how I wanna do that.” And they’ll always say, “Okay,
sure. Fine.” And then you can go back in and you can implement
a solution that is gonna actually strengthen the copy.
And then the fourth kind of crit, the one that you really want, is the
one where the client finds a problem in the copy, or an opportunity
in the copy, and has suggested [Audio Cuts Out] __________
solution ________ brilliant. When that happens, he’s just made
you money. Go ahead, jump on a plane. Fly up there and kiss him
on the cheek. He’s a great guy. Those are the kinds of crits that
you pray for. It’s important to be able to personally separate your

-- 24 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 25 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 25 of 36
own emotions or feelings about the copy from your objective
assessment of what will work and what won’t work.
And it’s enough, especially for young writers, to figure out what
you think is gonna work. It gets very easy to become confused
when a client or somebody else jumps into the mix and starts
giving you suggestions and ideas and crit. And it’s important to
keep your own sense of balance, and know that you’re responding
to the crit on an intellectual basis, not on an emotional one. You’re
not rejecting good crits because you’re feeling defensive. And
you’re not accepting bad crits because you’re intimidated. So it’s
very important to stay true to your copy.
Bob Bly: 	Makes sense. Now, next you promised the magic three-word
answer that can instantly end any copywriting argument and keep
your strongest effort intact. You’re having these discussions and
let’s say it’s not a good – you don’t like the solution the client has
suggested, or you don’t think the problem the client thinks is a
problem exists, and you’re not winning the argument. You’re
talking and she keeps saying, or he keeps saying, “Yeah but, yeah
but,” and they’re not agreeing with you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, the three words are “Let’s test it.” We used to do this at
Weiss all the time. I’ve done it with all my clients as a matter of
fact, where somebody will come along and say, “Well, this
headline is great but I’d like to see something like this instead.”
I’ll say, “Well, let’s test it.” Or one client always loved to tell me
what the market thinks. “Oh, when people see this they’ll think
that.” And they’ll just throw it away.
Well, he didn’t know what the market thought. He didn’t have a
clue. So [Laughs] I would just – rather than argue with them and
try to explain to him why the market didn’t think that, which is
silly because I didn’t know for sure what the market thought either.
I just had a good gut on it. I’d say, “Well, hey. Why don’t we test
it?” And more often than not, both of them worked quite well, and
when that happens, when you have two headlines that work well,
then what happens is the customer, the client, can bicycle the
headlines. So in January he mails Headline A, and in February he
mails Headline B.
And so then that means that your package doesn’t wear out and tire
as quickly. You don’t have that attrition when the same people see
the package for a second time because they’re seeing a fresh
headline, a fresh lead maybe. So it ends up making you money.

-- 25 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 26 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 26 of 36
“So let’s test it” is a really good way to solve major issues where
you just can’t come to a meeting of the mind with a client.
Bob Bly: 	Now, moving to what some might call the dark side of [Audio Cuts
Out] ___________ copywriting, generally seminars and
teleconferences like this and boot camps are very positive about
freelance writing, but there are problems with it, freelance
copywriting, just like there’re problems with any professions. So
next on our agenda is your five times when you should demand
your kill fee and kiss the client goodbye, walk away. That sounds
pretty radical. So when would you ever wanna do that?
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think I’ve only done it maybe once or twice in 33 years.
Bob Bly: 	So this is an infrequent episode?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, very. Well, in terms of just outright firing the client,
absolutely. I’ve had a client that took two months to get back to
me with a first set of crits. And then they were very – they were
way off the mark. It was obvious that the person I was working
with had no idea, and I found out later this was her first – the first
time they’d ever worked with a copywriter. And it was a larger
company, so that surprised me. If you sat around and waited for
clients to take that kind of time with every set of – with every
draft, I’d write one package a year.
And the package would take a year to get into the mail, and I’d be
making peanuts. So the client – I need a feeling from the client
that they’re as motivated to get this in the mail as I am. And that
means responding to crits and responding to research [Audio Cuts
Out] _________ in a timely manner.
Bob Bly: 	What’s timely? A day? A week? A month?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, it depends on what the request is. If you’re just asking for a
copy review, a week or so should be plenty. Sometimes these
companies go into budget meetings and administrative tasks for a
period of time and so it may be a couple of weeks, but certainly no
more than that. They want your copy on a deadline, and
presumably the reason is so they can get in the mail on deadline. If
there – their own worst enemy when it comes turning around crits
on draft. So that’s a major red flag. What you want as a
copywriter, the ideal client, is somebody who’ll take your first
draft, give you a few quick comments.

-- 26 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 27 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 27 of 36
They’ll make it legal, and maybe even strengthen the copy. Ram it
through design a couple weeks. Test it within six weeks, and roll
out in three months. That’s an ideal client because that means the
royalties you’re gonna earn on this will start coming in three
months down the line or, depending on how large your advance is,
very quickly thereafter. When you have a client that drags their
feet like that, you can’t expect the royalties to come in for a year.
Bob Bly: 	So No. 1, you might fire a client when they drag their feet on the
copy. They take too long to get back to you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep.
Bob Bly: 	What are some of the other situations?
Clayton Makepeace: 	There are times when you’ll find yourself doing – you’ll be with a
client who’ll be requesting these endless number of drafts, and
they just seem pointless. They just, “Well, I” –
Bob Bly: 	I know who you’re talking about.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I mean it just keeps going on and on and on, and I had a
client and it was a very profitable client for me for many years, and
I went back to something I had written for these people to see what
the last draft was on something that I’d written years earlier ’cause
I wanted to excerpt some of the copy, and I realized that I’d written
27 drafts. Now, granted they weren’t complete drafts. There
might be a change in the product or a change in the premium or
something like that –
Bob Bly: 	But you had to open the file and do work 27 times?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Twenty-seven times.
Bob Bly: 	So that’s no good.
Clayton Makepeace: 	And quite often, it wasn’t because of my copy, it was because the
editor kept changing his opinion of the economy, or the premium
kept changing, or the product kept changing, or new legal concerns
came up, or whatever. And so that kind of a client costs you a lot
of money. And if they’re making you a lot, that’s fine. But if
they’re not, then they go to the bottom of the list.
Bob Bly: 	Okay, so we don’t want clients that want 27 drafts?

-- 27 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 28 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 28 of 36
Clayton Makepeace: 	Um hm. Another one is – I have a friend, a copywriter friend, who
wrote a really great health package. And she gets it in the mail.
And it’s not her headline.
Bob Bly: 	I’ve had that happen.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Somebody rewrote the headline. And so she called the client and
she said, “What the heck happened? I had a kick-butt headline.
This thing’s wimpy. This package is in deep trouble. What
happened?” “Oh, well, we got to the press and the editor changed
his mind and didn’t like the headline, so we had to change it.”
Another case with a different writer, it was a legal issue that came
up at the last minute. Nobody called the writer. The copy was
changed. That client should be fired immediately. There’s no way
that you wanna work for a client, because then they’re gonna call
back in a few weeks and they’re gonna say, “Hey, look. Your
package didn’t work.” And my friend said, “What are you talking
about? It wasn’t my package.”
“That was your package. You guys changed the copy.”
Bob Bly: 	Exactly.
Clayton Makepeace: 	So anyone that does that is just absolute poison. They have to
respect a copywriter more than that. If they’re on the press at 2:00
in the morning and a lawyer says, “Hey, you gotta change this
word in the headline to that word in the headline,” I want a phone
call. And then I can say, “Well, that’s not gonna work. Why don’t
we use this word instead?” And we can hopefully come to some
resolution that’ll save the package.
Bob Bly: 	So avoid clients who rewrite your copy without your permission or
without telling you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	What else is so bad that you would walk away from a client?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I touched on this one earlier, and it’s basically the client who
thinks you’re taking dictation.
Bob Bly: 	What does that mean?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, when they give you crits, they seem shocked that you don’t
just go through the copy and make the changes they’ve requested

-- 28 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 29 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 29 of 36
verbatim. If you can get around that with them by careful care and
feeding of that client, then great. But if they’re insulted or angry
that you want to – that you won’t just take dictation from them,
then you’ve got a problem on your hands, and that client is going
to be less than productive for you. A lot of this we’re talking about
is dollar sense. So it’s just common sense. But a lot of it too is the
emotional side of it. As you’re building your copywriting career,
you’ll be doing more and more projects.
And you wanna love those projects, and you wanna be excited
about working on them and returning to them and keeping [Audio
Cuts Out] __________ fresh. And it’s just no fun when you’re
dealing with people who are changing your copy or who are
insulting to you, or who don’t value you and want you to just take
dictation or whatever. And so there’s an emotional side of this too.
I have a friend, I keep returning to this particular friend today for
some reason, but – who really has a good balance on this.
Basically the money has to be good, and I have to love the work.
If the money isn’t good but I love the work, I might hang around a
little bit just because I love the work. But if I hate the work, no
matter how good the money is, it’s just not worth it.
And then, of course, the final time to move on is when you find a
more lucrative client. When you find somebody that does any of
the above things better, that gets back to you quicker on drafts, that
collaborates better with you on the copy, that provides you with
better research, that gives you the freedom that you need in order
to produce winners, and someone who does all of those things is
going to be a more lucrative client for you. And if your schedule is
full or filling and you find somebody like that, then you’re going to
want to cut the bottom – the clients at the bottom of the list that are
less helpful in those ways. And so quite often through your career,
you’re going to have to make that choice. But I think it’s
important for copywriters to realize that we are businesspeople
first. And to constantly be sorting that client list and saying,
“Who’s the best in terms of both income potential and joy of
working for them? And who are the ones that I kinda dread the
phone call? I kinda dread working with? That’s difficult? And
also the ones that I tend not to do as well for.”
And constantly be sorting that list, and constantly be looking for
new people to replace the ones that’re at the bottom.
Bob Bly: 	That makes sense. Now, we’ve got a few questions. First is from
Joanne. Joanne says, “Where is the [Audio Cuts Out] __________

-- 29 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 30 of 36

of finding upcoming marketing conferences where I can network
with potential clients?”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Bob, maybe you’d be the help on that one. I draw a blank on that
because, as I said in the total package, I haven’t been a conference
animal.
Bob Bly: 	I’m not much of a network either, but let me give the answer and
tell what I do. I actually don’t like to network, so what I’ve done
to get myself out at these conferences, I actually don’t attend to
network. I will volunteer to give a presentation. That way, you’re
viewed as the expert, and all the attention for that hour of that
conference is focused on you. And you’re someone who didn’t
just go there to go get business, which makes you look sort of
needy, but you’re someone who’s there because you’re a top
person in your field and you’re knowledgeable in email marketing
or search engine optimization or whatever you’re talking about.
Now, really, the answer where to go to conferences depends on
what your market is. So, for example, if you are interested in
doing, as Clayton does, financial newsletters, well one place you
could go is The Money Show.
But if you’re interested in let’s say working for the type of clients
that I do a lot of work for, business-to-business type companies,
you would find an organization where the marketing managers of
those companies go. It happens to be the Business Marketing
Association, which is in Chicago. It’s marketing –
www.marketing.org. If you wanna do a lot of work in newsletters,
financial health newsletters, business newsletters, travel
newsletters, other publications – and newsletters as we’ve said is a
great market for copywriters – then you wanna get involved with
an organization called NEPA, the Newsletter and Electronic
Publishers Association.
And that’s at www.newsletters.org. So those are some sources for
you.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I think another one too – I think you make an excellent point on
going as a speaker. There’re a couple of things there. We will be
announcing our first conference here slated for next spring in the
next couple of issues of the total package. That’ll be an excellent
opportunity to network because we’ll have clients there as well as
copywriters, marketing people as well as writers. Another one too.
I’ve avoided this like the plague my entire life, but the DMA –
Bob Bly: 	I was just gonna say the DMA, we overlooked it. National –

-- 30 of 36 --
