Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah –
Bob Bly: 	Both national meetings that’re held in New York or Washington,
as well as your local chapters.
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Direct Marketing Association –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Implications for speakers so if you have – if you would like to
present on a particular aspect –
Bob Bly: 	And unless you hate the idea of public speaking, I think that’s
often a better way to go than just show up and be a networker.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep. Instead of being the teenage girl chasing rock star through a
lobby, you’re an authority on a particular subject –
[Crosstalk]
Bob Bly: 	Right. You’re sort of the rock star for the hour.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yep.
Bob Bly: 	So we’ve answered where to find these marketing conferences to
network with potential clients. Next question is from Mark – our
friend Mark K. – does it make sense to try and get clients by doing
spec assignments for companies that pay big and that you wanna
work for at the expense of a regular direct mail program for your
services? In other words, when time is limited and you’re in need
of clients, is one route better than the other? What he’s asking is
should I just contact a few big companies and offer to work for
them on spec? Or should I just do a mail blast and mail to 500
marketing directors and hope someone’s interested?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I always did all of the above, but my approach was to keep a
lot of lines in the water. We’re obviously talking about somebody
at the beginning of a career here who doesn’t already have a track
record and a history with these clients. Six steps that I took early
in my career to generate customers, to generate clients from a
fairly large list of big mailers that I was going after. And I did that
and that yield clients who – that I can trace every dollar I make to
today. And in addition to that, I [Audio Cuts Out] _________ took
the spec route. I also went to – I called it being a direct mail bully.

-- 31 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 32 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 32 of 36
I went to companies who’s controls I felt I could beat, and I did
one of several things.
Either I called them on the phone and offered them a deal where I
would write for free in return for a royalty if I beat their control, or
a bonus if they won’t pay royalties. And then I also would just go
away if I had time on my hands and felt inspired, like I could really
crank this out in a day or two, or in a relatively short period of
time, I’d just sit down and I’d write a kick-butt new package for
the company and send it to them. And then say, “Let me know
what you think. I wanna do this for you. And I’d be more than
happy to finish it up.” And then if they like what they see, then
chances are you’ve got a deal there. I would then negotiate either
some advance or some fee for finishing the package.
But now you’re in the door.
Bob Bly: 	I think that answer [Audio Cuts Out] _____________ don’t think
of these, Mark, as either or. People will say to me, “Well, should I
write articles or should I send out mailings?” And I say, “Why is it
either or? You should write articles and you should send out
mailings.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	Right.
Bob Bly: 	Until you’re established and have – until you’re at the point where
more people are coming to you than you can handle. When you’re
not at that point, then you have the time to do multiple
self-promotion activities, and you should.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Bob, I’ve had several letters come into me too that I’d like to
address –
Bob Bly: 	Sure –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Real quickly if we can. We seem to have a few minutes left.
Bob Bly: 	We do.
Clayton Makepeace: 	One of the emails that I get over and over again is, you know, it’s
like it’s from people who’re looking for excuses why they can’t
succeed in this business. And the excuse is never their copy. They
have to do with barriers to entry. I got a letter the other day from a
woman who wanted to know is there sexual discrimination in
direct marketing?

-- 32 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 33 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 33 of 36
Bob Bly: 	I think I had the same one. Mine’s from Carrie.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Well, I wouldn’t [Laughs] –
Bob Bly: 	Well, we’re only giving first names so we’re not –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Okay, good. All right.
Bob Bly: 	Okay.
Clayton Makepeace: 	I’ve had several from people in Europe wanting to know if there’s
a barrier to non-Americans writing for the American market. I’ve
had –
Bob Bly: 	I get that a lot, but I’d like to hear how you answer that.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, and I’ve had people ask me if there’s race discrimination
going on. And let’s take ’em one at a time. Sexual discrimination.
Absolutely not. Not even a shred of it have I encountered or seen
in this marketplace. For one thing, most – and of course, we’re
talking about men not wanting to hire women here – in the first
place, most of the people who do the hiring and most of the
marketing managers are women. So all my bosses are women
almost.
I think I have maybe one or two clients who I’m working with a
male marketing director. The rest of them are all women. So I
think it would be kind of silly for the [Audio Cuts Out]
discriminate against their own just on the basis of sex. And I have
friends who happen to be females who are doing extremely well in
this market. You all have read about Carline Anglade-Cole, and
you’ve all probably heard me talk about Kim Krause. Well, these
are two wonderful writers who create kick-butt controls for their
clients. They’re well known as A-level writers, and they make a
fortune.
So if you’re looking for an excuse, sexual discrimination will not
be one of them. It doesn’t hold water. On the racial discrimination
thing, the same thing holds. There’s no reason why a person of
any ethnic background, why a response or money or customers
generated by a person of any ethnic background would be less
valuable to a client than people that are – people and money that
are generated by another writer. So again, these people’s whole
lives turn on finding great writers. People who can reduce results.
You produce results and it doesn’t matter really who you are.

-- 33 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 34 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 34 of 36
As far as the country discrimination is concerned, we’re obviously
talking about people from Great Britain and Australia and Canada
and other countries that are English speaking, wanting to know are
there barriers to them to entering? And the only barrier there really
– there is something to consider there; it’s not really a barrier. And
that is – that’s just – when a sales copy rewrite is colloquial in
nature, in other words we’re not following all the great rules of
grammar and so forth. We’re using idioms and we’re using
metaphors, similes that our prospects are familiar with, and those
figures of speech are quite often not familiar to people from other
countries, or as familiar as they are to us. And so sometimes when
you’re working with writers from other countries, there’ll be minor
issues crop up in the copy. British are famous for calling the trunk
of a car the boot. You know? Just things like that. They can be
easily handled though.
I would encourage people from other countries to immerse
themselves in American culture as much as possible to get a handle
for the differences in our languages. But having said that, people
are the same everywhere, and the things that motivate us are the
same. And so I encourage people from other countries to take a
whack at the American market. In fact, I have a man from the
Netherlands right now writing for me, and another gentleman from
Canada writing for me. And I’m very happy so far with the work
they’ve done for me. So it’s overcomeable.
Bob Bly: 	What about is there age discrimination? I get often emails from
people who say, “I wanna have a second career. I’m interested in
copywriting, but I’m 60. I’m 65. I’m 70.”
Clayton Makepeace: 	I don’t think so. I was surprised and delighted at the AWAI
conference at the number of people who’re making copywriting a
second career. And I think it’s wonderful. I think it’s great that
that’s happening. I can see that when a marketing manager is
hiring a young person who’s just starting off, they don’t have the
impression that this person has moved on from something else.
They’re kinda like fresh, right?
Bob Bly: 	Right.
Clayton Makepeace: 	When you go to hire a 50-year-old that has no experience or very
little experience in the marketplace, there might be some
reservation at first, but remember, the copy that you’re writing, the
samples that you’re showing, that speaks volumes. That’s what
it’s all about. And it’s really not about your age or whether this is
your second or third or fourth career, or your first. It’s about the

-- 34 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 35 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 35 of 36
power of the words that you put on the page. And so if those
words are powerful – you know, this is all about self-interest for
the clients. If you can sell their product, they want you. That’s the
bottom line.
If you can sell their product as well as or better than they are now,
then they’re going to line up to get on your dance card. That’s all
there is to it.
Bob Bly: 	Terrific. Do you have other questions there in front of you?
Clayton Makepeace: 	Yeah, I just wanted to – there was a question that I received about
other – are there any other ways to get compensation? I just
wanted to share one that I tried once and it worked out quite well
for me. A particular client wanted me to work with him, but for
one reason or another the upfront arrangement that I was used to
just didn’t work that well for him. And so what I did was I
structured a deal where I could work for less on the front end,
certainly enough to cover my monthly nut, but I took stock options
in his company. Because I was absolutely convinced that I could
quadruple the size of that company. And so I went and I said,
“Okay, look. I need this much to live every month and to live
well, but that’s not – and that’s basically all you can afford right
now. But if I didn’t come to work for you full-time or exclusively
with you, I would be making this much.
Many, many, many times more. So if I’m going to [Audio Cuts
Out] _________ make this investment in your company, I want an
option that says that X years down the road, three is – whatever
you think it’ll take you to really produce the growth – three years
down the road I want stock options worth X percent of your
company. And today those stock options are worthless, but I’m
just going to – the money that I’ll get when I cash those options in
will compensate me for this time that I’m spending with you. And
he went for it. And it was a nice very, very healthy payday for me
when I cashed those options out. So be creative about these deals.
You may find somebody that would only use that kind of approach
on some – with a client that I was absolutely convinced was set up
for explosive growth, and the missing ingredient was me.
And if I find a client like that, then I’m more than willing to take
that kind of a deal.
Bob Bly: 	Terrific. And on that note, we are at the end of our hour and a half
for today. So Clayton, we wanna thank you once again. Next
week’s call is No. 3 in the series titled “Write better copy faster,

-- 35 of 36 --

The Profit Center 	3_How_to_Keep_Your_Clients_Coming_Back 	Page 36 of 36
Clayton Makepeace, Bob Bly, Wendy Makepeace, Julie McManus
www.escriptionist.com 	Page 36 of 36
produce up to twice as many projects per year, and get bigger
winners more often.” Which is something we all wanna learn.
And that is to dial into Wednesday at noon like they always are.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Great.
Wendy Makepeace: 	And –
Clayton Makepeace: 	Thanks a lot –
Bob Bly: 	Thanks everybody –
Wendy Makepeace: 	Great. Bye-bye –
Bob Bly: 	Bye.
Clayton Makepeace: 	Bye-bye –
[End of Audio]
